title: SQL Basics — SELECT & WHERE
description: Core SQL querying: SELECT statements, WHERE filtering, NULL handling, and basic operators.

questions.text: Which SQL statement is used to retrieve data from a database?
What does the following query return? SELECT * FROM Employees

options.text: Only the first column of the Employees table
All rows and all columns from the Employees table
The number of rows in the Employees table
Only the primary key column of the Employees table

questions.text: Which clause is used to filter rows returned by a SELECT statement?
What is the correct syntax to select only the 'Name' and 'Salary' columns from a table called 'Staff'?

options.text: SELECT Name AND Salary FROM Staff

questions.text: Which keyword is used to give a column a temporary alias in a query result? Example: SELECT Salary AS MonthlySalary FROM Staff
What does SELECT DISTINCT City FROM Customers return?

options.text: The city that appears most often

questions.text: Which operator checks if two values are NOT equal in SQL?
What does the following query return? SELECT * FROM Orders WHERE Amount > 500

options.text: All orders where Amount is at least 500
All orders where Amount is exactly 500
All orders where Amount is greater than 500 (excluding 500)
All orders where Amount is less than 500

questions.text: Which of the following correctly checks whether the column 'MiddleName' contains no value (is NULL)?
Which query finds all employees who DO have a value in the 'Email' column?

options.text: SELECT * FROM Employees WHERE Email != NULL
SELECT * FROM Employees WHERE Email IS NOT NULL
SELECT * FROM Employees WHERE Email <> NULL
SELECT * FROM Employees WHERE NOT Email = NULL

questions.text: What does the BETWEEN operator do? Example: WHERE Price BETWEEN 10 AND 50

options.text: Returns rows where Price is strictly between 10 and 50 (exclusive)
Returns rows where Price is between 10 and 50 (inclusive of both endpoints)
Returns rows where Price is greater than 10 only
Returns rows where Price equals either 10 or 50

questions.text: Which query selects all products with a CategoryID of 1, 2, or 3?

options.text: SELECT * FROM Products WHERE CategoryID = 1 OR 2 OR 3
SELECT * FROM Products WHERE CategoryID IN (1, 2, 3)
SELECT * FROM Products WHERE CategoryID BETWEEN 1 AND 3 INCLUSIVE

questions.text: Which query returns all customers whose name does NOT start with 'A'?

options.text: SELECT * FROM Customers WHERE Name NOT LIKE 'A%'
SELECT * FROM Customers WHERE Name NOT = 'A%'

questions.text: In a LIKE pattern, what does the '%' wildcard represent?

options.text: Zero or more characters of any type

questions.text: In a LIKE pattern, what does the '_' (underscore) wildcard represent?
Which query finds all products with names that have exactly 5 characters?

options.text: SELECT * FROM Products WHERE Name LIKE '%%%%%'
SELECT * FROM Products WHERE Name LIKE '_____'
Both b and c are correct

questions.text: How do you write a single-line comment in SQL?
How do you write a multi-line comment in SQL?

options.text: -- This is line 1 -- This is line 2

questions.text: What is wrong with this query? SELECT * FROM Employees WHERE Department = NULL

options.text: Nothing is wrong; it will correctly find rows with NULL Department
NULL cannot be compared with = ; use IS NULL instead
The FROM clause is missing the schema name
SELECT * cannot be used with WHERE

questions.text: Which keyword eliminates duplicate rows from a query result?
What does this query return? SELECT * FROM Products WHERE Price >= 100 AND Price <= 200

options.text: Products where Price is less than 100 or greater than 200
Products where Price is exactly 100 or exactly 200
Products where Price is between 100 and 200 inclusive
Products where Price is greater than 100 and less than 200 exclusive

questions.text: A column alias defined with AS in a SELECT list — can it be referenced in the WHERE clause of the same query?

options.text: No, aliases defined in SELECT cannot be used in the WHERE clause of the same query
Only if the alias is in double quotes

questions.text: Which query selects employees NOT in departments 10, 20, or 30?

options.text: SELECT * FROM Employees WHERE DeptID NOT IN (10, 20, 30)
SELECT * FROM Employees WHERE DeptID EXCLUDE (10, 20, 30)
SELECT * FROM Employees WHERE DeptID != IN (10, 20, 30)

questions.text: What does SELECT 1 + 1 return in standard SQL (without a FROM clause in databases that support it)?

options.text: An error — you must always specify a FROM clause

questions.text: Which of the following LIKE patterns would match the string 'Smith'?
What is the mandatory clause that must follow every SELECT in a standard SQL query targeting a table?
If a column contains NULL, which comparison returns TRUE? WHERE Score = NULL

options.text: TRUE — NULL equals NULL in SQL
FALSE — NULL comparisons with = always return UNKNOWN/FALSE
It depends on the database engine

questions.text: Which query correctly uses a table alias? SELECT e.Name, e.Salary FROM Employees e WHERE e.Salary > 5000

options.text: The query is invalid because aliases must use AS keyword
The query is valid; 'e' is an alias for the Employees table
The query is invalid because aliases can only be used on columns
The query is valid but the alias 'e' is ignored

questions.text: What does NOT IN return when the sublist contains a NULL value? Example: WHERE ID NOT IN (1, 2, NULL)

options.text: It works normally, ignoring the NULL
It returns no rows because NOT IN with NULL always evaluates to UNKNOWN

questions.text: Which of the following is TRUE about SELECT * in production SQL code?

options.text: SELECT * is always recommended for simplicity
SELECT * is generally discouraged in production because it returns unnecessary columns and can break if schema changes
SELECT * is faster than selecting named columns
